race.name: Tour de Big Bear
race.tagline: 100 miles and 8,500 feet of climbing at 7,000-foot altitude through the San Bernardino Mountains. Onyx Summit at 8,443 feet is the highest paved road in Southern California.

vitals.registration: Online via tourdebigbear.com. Tiered pricing with increases throughout the year. Approximately $100-$150 for the 100-mile course.
vitals.feed_zones: Legendary aid stations with full buffets, DJ booths, BBQ ribs, couch-lounging rest areas, and live music. On-course mechanics and SAG support.
vitals.cutoff_time: 100-mile riders must reach Blue Sky Meadows Aid Station by 12:30 PM or be rerouted to the 70-mile course
route_options: 100 miles: The Climb (8,500+ ft elevation gain)

climate.primary: Southern California mountain summer at 7,000+ feet
climate.description: August at 7,000 feet in the San Bernardino Mountains delivers spectacular summer weather that is dramatically different from the lowland SoCal heat. Morning temperatures at the start are 55-65F, rising to 75-85F by midday. The altitude moderates what would be 100F+ temperatures at lower elevations. The air is dry with intense UV radiation. Afternoon thunderstorms are possible but not common in early August. The descent to lower elevations on the 100-mile course can push temperatures above 90F before the return climb to altitude.
challenges: 7,000+ foot altitude throughout: reduced oxygen for sea-level riders
Temperature swing from 55F at start to 90F+ at lower elevation descents
Intense UV radiation at altitude requires sunscreen and hydration
Possible afternoon thunderstorms in the San Bernardino Mountains

terrain.primary: Paved mountain roads through San Bernardino National Forest
terrain.surface: 100% paved on mountain highways and forest roads. Highway 18 (Rim of the World) and Highway 38 are well-maintained two-lane mountain roads with some rough patches and narrow shoulders. The Onyx Summit climb on Highway 38 is the centerpiece: sustained climbing to 8,443 feet. Descents are fast with tight switchbacks on the Rim of the World section. Pine-scented forest roads alternate with exposed high-desert sections.
features: Onyx Summit: highest paved road in Southern California at 8,443 feet
Rim of the World Scenic Byway: Highway 18 with panoramic views
Big Bear Lake circuit: alpine lake at 6,752 feet
Cedar Falls descent and return climb

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 69. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 69.

course_description.character: A deceptive course that lulls you with rolling lakeside roads for 50 miles before delivering the altitude hammer in the second half. The Onyx Summit climb at 8,443 feet is the crown jewel, and the Cedar Falls descent-and-return is where tired legs at altitude learn about humility.
course_description.signature_challenge: Onyx Summit at 8,443 feet. The highest paved road in Southern California, reached after 50+ miles of riding at altitude. The KOM/QOM competition makes the climb honest. Then you descend 2,000 feet to Cedar Falls and must climb back up.

logistics.airport: Ontario International (ONT) 75 miles, 2 hours. Los Angeles International (LAX) 110 miles, 2.5 hours. San Diego (SAN) 130 miles, 2.5 hours.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Book Big Bear Lake accommodation early. Visit Big Bear (visitbigbear.com) lists cabins, condos, hotels, and vacation homes. Stay on the lake or near Bear Mountain Resort. Cabins are the local specialty and range from rustic to luxury. Prices are more reasonable than coastal resort towns: $100-$300/night.
logistics.food: Big Bear Village has casual mountain dining: burgers, pizza, BBQ, and brewpubs. Peppercorn Grille is the local favorite for upscale. Nottingham's Tavern for post-ride beer. But honestly, the aid stations on course will feed you so well that dinner plans may change.

history.origin_story: The Tour de Big Bear launched in 2010, the same year Big Bear Lake hosted the AMGEN Tour of California. The Big Bear Cycling Association saw an opportunity to establish a permanent cycling event that would leverage the mountain town's natural advantages: altitude, stunning scenery, ideal summer weather, and roads tested by professional cycling. The event grew rapidly from a modest first edition to 2,500+ riders across multiple distances. The legendary aid stations became the event's signature, with local volunteers transforming rest stops into mini-festivals. Named the #1 Gran Fondo in North America by Gran Fondo Guide in 2023.
notable_moments: 2010: Inaugural Tour de Big Bear launches alongside AMGEN Tour of California at Big Bear
2023: Named #1 Gran Fondo in North America by Gran Fondo Guide
2024: Moves to Bear Mountain Resort as permanent start/finish venue
2025: Adds Dirty Burro 50K XC mountain bike race and Dirty Bear/Dirty Cub gravel events
2025: Jonas Woodruff wins 100-mile in 4:31:54, Thomas Sullivan 1 second back
2026: Event expands to 3-day format with Thursday check-in, Friday festival, Saturday rides
history.reputation: Southern California's favorite ride. North America's #1 Gran Fondo (2023). Known for legendary aid stations and altitude challenge.

biased_opinion.summary:
The Tour de Big Bear is what happens when a Southern California cycling community builds their dream ride: 100 miles at 7,000 feet with 8,500 feet of climbing, legendary aid stations that serve BBQ ribs and have DJ booths, and a mountain lake setting that delivers 55-65F start temperatures while the rest of SoCal melts in August.

The climbing is real. Onyx Summit at 8,443 feet is the highest paved road in Southern California, and the KOM competition up its slopes draws genuine efforts. The profile is back-loaded and punishing: rolling lakeside roads lull you for 50 miles, then the Onyx push and Cedar Falls loop deliver the hurt. The 12:30 PM cutoff at Blue Sky Meadows is there for a reason -- undertrained riders bonk at altitude.

But the aid stations are what make this event legendary. Full buffets, DJs, couches, ribs, live music. The volunteers are locals who have been doing this for 15 years and take genuine pride in their hospitality. Named the #1 Gran Fondo in North America by Gran Fondo Guide in 2023. If you are a SoCal cyclist, this is your annual pilgrimage.
strengths: 8,500 feet of climbing peaking at Onyx Summit (8,443 ft) -- the highest paved road in SoCal
Legendary aid stations: BBQ ribs, DJ booths, couches, full buffets, live music
7,000+ foot altitude delivers ideal summer weather while lowland SoCal swelters
3-day festival: Glow Ride, Poker Ride, Expo, Recovery Rides, road/gravel/MTB events
weaknesses: 2-2.5 hour drive from LA on winding mountain roads creates logistical friction
7,000+ foot altitude is a genuine performance limiter for sea-level riders
100-mile course has strict 12:30 PM cutoff that turns back slow riders
Primarily a SoCal regional event with limited national/international draw
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you live in Southern California and you have not done the Tour de Big Bear, you are missing the best gran fondo in the state. 100 miles at altitude with aid stations that belong in a restaurant. Go.

final_verdict.one_liner: 100 miles at 7,000 feet with legendary aid stations that serve ribs, have DJs, and make you forget you are dying on a bike.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want the definitive SoCal mountain cycling experience with altitude, climbing, and aid stations that redefine hospitality. Skip if you cannot handle 7,000+ foot altitude, need a sanctioned race, or live far from California.
final_verdict.alternatives: For a similar SoCal climbing challenge: Mammoth Gran Fondo (higher altitude, less festival). For competitive SoCal road racing: Belgian Waffle Ride. For a NorCal mountain century: Levi's GranFondo.

videos.title: I Bonked HARD - 2022 Tour de Big Bear
videos.description:
A couple weeks back my teammates and I went up to Big Bear Lake, CA to tackle the 2022 Tour de Big Bear. This is a Gran Fondo that is has multiple offerings, however, we chose the century ride option  with around 8,000 feet of climbing and just over 100 miles. 

Although this is not a proper bike race I decided I wanted to stay with the lead group for as long as possible before getting shelled so that I could keep up the overall pace and ideally complete this ride faster than any century ride I've done in the past. 

Of course, with folks like Phil Gaimon and other legit pros on this ride, that was going to be next to impossible. 

We hung with the lead group for most of the day and the team really did ride this like it was a road race for the most part. 

My nutrition plan broke down, HARD and I didn't really recover. I didn't follow the on bike nutrition targets I had planned for myself and I paid for it about 70 miles in when I bonked and had no glycogen stores left for my body to run on.

I learned some valuable lessons from the 2022 Tour de Big Bear so hopefully you can learn from my mistakes. 

Let me know what you thought of the video in the comments below!

Also, if you're heading out to Big Bear again next year or have any other fondo recommendations for the off season give me a holler I'd love to tackle them and document the experience. 

Learn more about my daily life and cycling in San Diego:

Connect with me on Strava: https://www.strava.com/athletes/41838530
Follow my Instagram: https://www.instagram.com/daltontberry/
tags: tour de big bear gran fondo
videos.transcript:
what is happening ladies and gentlemen
this year we took part in the 2022 tour
day big bear this happens just a couple
hours north of san diego in big bear
lake california the tour de big bear
offers a wide variety of options for a
more family friendly inclusive grand
fondo type environment the rides range
from like a family fun ride to a 25 mile
or 50 mile or 70 miler and a big boy 100
mile ride that me and the teammates did
just this last weekend on saturday in
addition to that this year i don't know
if it's happened in past years but this
year they had a 25 mile and a 50 mile
gravel option i've written a lot of
really cool routes in big bear before
shout out to phil gaiman for sending me
those routes via email a couple years
back since then i've tackled quite a few
of them some of my favorites being onyx
peak jinx lake amongst others since
there's not really a lot of road racing
going on anymore in southern california
this is a really good opportunity for us
to get out and ride as a team and almost
do a fake race i guess you would say so
today i want to walk you through our
experience at torta big bear talk to you
about how we rode this ride and then
ultimately break down the route a little
bit what i really liked about this grand
fondo and then maybe what i would
improve in the years coming so obviously
guys going into this it's not an
official race there are times segments
there's a kom and qom competition that
is actually taking place up the front
side of onyx peak and it ends at the
summit this kom competition takes your
time up the whole grade it starts you at
the bottom as soon as you start the
ascent and then whoever is the fastest
up that over the course of the day wins
some cool prizes i have no idea what
those prizes were so guys starting out
this ride it was not that early of a
morning for us it was 7 am we did ride
to the course it was about 4 miles all
downhill to get there if you are gonna
be dead at the end of the 100 miles like
i was i definitely would not recommend
doing that just drive to the start line
but make sure to get there early so you
can find parking in the general vicinity
because it does fill up fast now in the
days before there was an expo we did not
attend the expo i did hear that there
was live music so that's a cool touch
that the big bear organizers had thrown
in there for those of you that are maybe
bringing the family or have people in
your family that are non-cyclists that
want something to do
kind of related to bikes but maybe not a
hundred percent related to bikes the
start of this ride is chaotic i really
recommend if you're an experienced
cyclist to get there early and get as
close to the front as possible
we got there a little late i got in
really late on friday night and i had to
reg that morning so i popped over got my
wristband got my information got my
placard and everything and then i popped
over to the start line but by the time i
got there it was already jam-packed in
the front when you start this ride you
have to zigzag through a bunch of
neighborhoods it's really hard to move
up we saw almost two crashes within the
first like five minutes of the ride so
immediately i kind of went on the attack
and just slotted up as far as i could to
the front group because that's where i
really wanted to remain for as long as
possible until at the very least i got
popped out of that and then got thrown
back to the second or third or fourth or
maybe even the last group i don't know
but starting in the front with
experienced cyclists if you are
experienced or a racer or something
along those lines is always a good idea
if you start towards the back and you're
not concerned about getting in with the
front group then give yourself a little
bit of space so you're not crossing up
wheels and you're not going to go down
and take out 100 people with you once
you clear all the neighborhoods they let
you out on your own and then it's kind
of a free-for-all everybody kept it
pretty chill on this 100 mile ride to
start things off but it wasn't long
before the pace got hot as we were
coming up on the first climb legendary
phil guyman comes flying by us and takes
over the front i was really happy with
the leisurely pace we were rolling at 25
26 27 miles an hour over and up and down
and around the rollers and i was keeping
my power in check unfortunately when
phil passed us that was no longer an
option so we hit the first climb i
immediately looked down and i was doing
600 watts this was definitely not ideal
and i knew i needed to pace myself and i
was even gonna plan on making it a full
100 miles with 8 000 feet of climbing
that being said i still kind of sent it
up the first climb thinking that i
wanted to stick with this front group
and maybe i would catch some folks on
the descent i did drop back though i
grabbed spencer zach and colin who came
with me on this ride and i made sure
that we were all linked up because the
whole point at the end of the day was to
ride as a team and get in a really solid
training ride while we weren't the
climbiest of boys we're all pretty large
compared to a lot of people when we
raced crits which are completely flat
we actually were able to do all right
and we kept it slow and steady and we
picked off a bunch of people on that
first climb at the end of the first
couple descents we were able to gain
some time and rotate together to catch
up to the second group which was also
the main chase this was all fine and
dandy until colin had to go pee we made
the fatal error of stopping at the
second rest station and nobody else in
our group stopped at that rest station
we thought we could be super quick get
in and out go pee and then chase back on
but unfortunately the pace was just way
too hot to do that we found ourselves
jumping in with a third group but at
this time not a lot of folks in that
group were motivated to pull so we ended
up as a team on the front doing a
rotation and unfortunately i was too
dumb to realize that i probably
shouldn't be taking three to four
hundred watt pools and doing 28 miles an
hour for
probably 45 minutes to an hour it was
really cool to have the team organized
and all in one place and riding together
like this is really going to help us in
future road races and future criterium
racing pretty much any other time that
we ride together in training rides as
well especially when we're in a big
group it's all mixed up and it's not
just us out on the road doing a training
ride it was nice to ride with a bunch of
other people this route could not have
been more scenic or more beautiful it
starts you out with a little out and
back along the lakeside you snake
through underneath all the trees under
the canopy it's absolutely gorgeous and
this time of year the weather turned out
to be perfect even though it was
originally calling for some rain i'll be
honest i feel like we could have gone
without some of the neighborhood snake
throughs that were on this route because
i feel like what was happening is the
organizers were just trying to get us as
close to 100 miles as possible i felt
like this was just a waste of mileage
when we could have just tacked it on
somewhere else or make the ride five
miles shorter it's not going to kill
anybody the next step is to head up to
onyx peak which is actually my absolute
favorite climb in all of the big bear
area there's about a 30 minute climb and
i got absolutely waxed in the kom
competition but i will say zach did
pretty well i think he took around 20th
or something like that in the overall
results so kudos to him for kicking butt
so the boys all waited for me at the top
of onyx peak which was super awesome as
i arrived probably two or three minutes
after them and i got passed by plenty of
people but i also did plenty of passing
myself so it was kind of weird didn't
really know what position i was in
overall but i do know that the group
that we were in before had kind of
exploded all over the mountain and this
was sort of the come to jesus moment
where everybody had to decide whether
they were going to try and continue
hammering or they were just going to
chill out and do a little zone 2 ride to
finish this thing out we did stop at the
rest station at the top thank goodness i
did not have near enough carbs near
enough calories or anything like that at
this rest station ironically enough i
left the house with nine gels and i came
home with six that is a problem the
descent was an absolute
ripper this descent is almost 12 miles
or something like that and we averaged
42.6 miles an hour for about 10 minutes
we actually came in like
12th 13th 14th and 15th overall on the
segment all time that's with like legit
world tour prototypes descending this
and everything else and on the day i
think we were second third fourth and
fifth fastest even faster than the lead
group
so we like to send it downhills but we
get our butts kick going uphill who'da
thunk nonetheless we did gain quite a
bit of time here so we were on a mission
to chase everything back however as soon
as we started hitting rollers i knew
something was really really really
really really wrong with my legs we had
been averaging about 22 to 23 miles an
hour up to this point and we were about
three and a half four hours in i knew we
had at least an hour hour and a half
left and we were at like mile 75 at this
point so we had quite a bit of time left
to ride and i knew that i was in trouble
because
i was not nutritionified the guys were
so far ahead of me that i thought it was
a good idea to skip the next rest
station and try and chase them down this
is where i actually crushed two more
gels and then i ate some salt stick
whatever electrolyte shoes i also
chugged an entire bottle thinking that
could help me out a little bit as well
and unfortunately it did not do as much
as i needed it to at this point i was
really only able to turn like 180 watts
at 160 bpm which is pretty darn close to
my threshold on top of that we're at
elevation of like seven thousand or
eight thousand feet so your threshold is
already probably just
chopped like 20 or something absurd like
that thank goodness about three miles
from the summit of onyx there is an aid
station so i linked up with colin there
and eli with bros ride bikes shout out
to those two popped in with them had a
coke was pretty cracked and it was
getting hot out there i was sweating out
all my sodium electrolytes whatever else
might be coming out of my system and i
really had nothing left in the tank so
it was at this point that i knew i was
getting really really close to bunking
and i needed to refuel unfortunately i
did not refuel near enough because i was
already way behind on my nutrition over
the first four and a half five hours of
the ride the last three miles of this
climb were perhaps the most brutal three
miles i have ever done in my life my
wattage just continued to drop i got
down to like 140 watts at almost 155 or
160 bpm my normal tempo is probably
sitting somewhere closer to 240 250 and
at this point i'm pedaling tempo five
hours into a ride at around 140 so i had
lost 100 watts obviously part of that is
due to duration but i think most of it
is due to nutrition and then probably a
little bit of the lack of training that
i've been undergoing because i was sick
twice last month once we finally crested
onyx i saw the rest of the squad and i
just kept rolling because i knew they
would catch me anyway and well
who wants to ride as slow as i was
riding at that point my left leg was
extremely locked up from cramping in my
quad and honestly the descent hurt just
as bad as the climb did but luckily the
descent i can still keep my speed up
pretty high even though i was in a lot
of pain once i got to the bottom i was
out there in the flats by myself and
once again we turned into the
neighborhoods to do some weird ziggy
zaggy stuff and once again guys could
have done without that one the last 20
miles probably took me like almost two
hours it was at this time the boys snuck
up on me they said what's up and they
kind of towed me to the line but
the saga isn't over yet my left cleat
yeah it was toast i don't know what
happened it broke or something like that
but i could no longer clip in on my left
side and that didn't help my cause we
finished 100 mile ride with 8 000 feet
of climbing and now let's talk about the
things that i learned the first lesson
here is eat the nutrition that you had
planned i took nine gels with me i
should have eaten all nine gels i took
like four or five clip bars i should
have had every single cliff bar that i
also had in my pockets in addition on
the nutrition front
eat more at the age stations they're
there for a reason this is a supported
ride and you paid for it make your stops
quick but also get in there and get the
right fuel for the ride it is a hundred
miles and you are on the bike for about
five hours if you're hauling ass the aid
stations i will say had plenty of stuff
to go around i was probably just not
seeing straight because i didn't see
half the stuff they did have but after
the ride the teammates and everybody
else was telling me that they had a ton
of options i guess they had fondue and i
never even saw this lesson three
maybe sticking with the lead lead group
isn't the best idea right off the bat so
know your limits sticking with the lead
groups for as long as possible is a good
idea because you get much more of the
ride done
very very quickly once we drop back to
group 3 or group 4 on the road we
realize very quickly that not that many
people there either wanted to or could
work with you to keep the pace hot and
to keep you within striking distance of
that second group if we wanted to stop
the p
maybe i wouldn't have done so much work
trying to chase back on and
realistically maybe i wouldn't have
bonked so early in the ride and i would
have had legs to pedal to the finish a
little bit harder and had a much better
time another thing i will say is take
part in the expo it's always nice to
show up chitchat with the people you
know that share a common interest with
you instead of just running off back to
the cabin with the team kind of like we
did now we did get in good hot tub hours
we had a good dinner at el jacalito
right down in the village and then we
went over to murray's for some karaoke
that was all a grand old time and i
highly recommend hanging out in big bear
and stay in the night overnight it's
already open for 2023 it's like august
5th and 6th 85 bucks way cheaper than
what i ended up paying last minute this
year if you guys want to see more
adventures like the tour day big bear or
you want to see some of the late season
racing i have coming up stay tuned
subscribe to the channel like this video
if you want to show support to the
channel and i will see you guys again
soon
[Music]

videos.curation_reason: Detailed race recap with specific insights about course logistics, KOM competition, and race strategy for the century ride.

videos.title: Tour de Big Bear Century Ride 2024 - 100 miles 8,000 ft of Climbing
videos.description:
Back at it again with the white Vans. We hit Big Bear Lake up and it hit back. This event is near and dear to my heart, as it was one of the first big events that got me into road cycling. This year was our 1st time riding the 100 mile course, and it didn't disappoint. 

The first half of the ride went pretty smooth and fast, we arrived at the halfway point at the top of Onyx in just around 3 hrs. The second half was quite a bit tougher. I messed up my fueling, got a stomach ache, and then leg cramps from not enough salt. Luckily my friends were there to support me and we all rolled in through the finish line together.

I highly recommend this ride, whatever distance you choose. The scenery is beautiful and it's a good time. 

Elapsed Time: 8hrs 4min
Moving Time: 6hrs 37min

videos.title: TOUR DE BIG BEAR 25’  SO. CAL’S  BEST FONDO !! First race on Canyon Aeroad CFR
videos.description: i rode in a supportive role for her and thoroughly enjoyed it. especially when she finished top 10 for women despite the slow and rough start due to the damn cold.

videos.title: Challenge Accepted - 100 mile Tour de Big Bear 2025 Cycling POV
videos.description:
Tour de Big Bear 2025 did not disappoint! A mix of both scenery and misery! The climbs and descents are paired with stunning views! If you haven't done a ride like this i encourage you to try it! It will push you to your limit but the reward is so worth it!

By the way, I signed up for the 100 mile ride with 8,000 ft elevation. 

Check out my ride on Strava: https://strava.app.link/vH35jQtWFVb

videos.curation_reason: Most recent 2025 first-person POV of the full century ride with detailed course footage and commentary.

quotes.text: We arrived at the halfway point at the top of Onyx in just around 3 hrs. The second half was quite a bit tougher. I messed up my fueling, got a stomach ache, and then leg cramps.

This is a KOM competition that is actually taking place up the front side of Onyx Peak and it ends at the summit. This KOM competition takes your time up the whole grade it starts you at the bottom as soon as you start the ascent and then whoever is the fastest up that over the course of the day wins some cool prizes.
We went down for like 30 minutes and as you know what comes down must come up or vice versa. So we will see. Right now we're going down but there's more climbing up ahead.

key_challenges.name: Onyx Pass
key_challenges.description: The first big climb that riders specifically mention as challenging. Features a KOM/QOM competition timing segment that starts at the bottom of the ascent and ends at the summit.

key_challenges.name: Onyx Summit
key_challenges.description: Described as brutal climbing. Riders mention conserving energy for the long ride back up after descending for 30 minutes.

key_challenges.name: Cedar Falls
key_challenges.description: The turnaround point where riders report feeling pretty dead. Still has 4,000 ft of climbing left with about 30 miles to go.

terrain_notes.text: Riders describe having to zigzag through neighborhoods at the start, making it hard to move up in position.
The course includes sections around Big Bear Lake with beautiful scenery on the north and northeast sides of the lake.

race_day_tips.text: Get there early and get as close to the front as possible if you're an experienced cyclist. The start is chaotic and it's really hard to move up once you're in the neighborhoods.
Drive to the start line rather than riding if you think you'll be dead at the end of 100 miles. Make sure to get there early to find parking because it fills up fast.
If starting towards the back, give yourself space so you don't cross up wheels - riders saw almost two crashes within the first five minutes.
Conserve energy for the climbs. After descending for 30 minutes, there's still brutal climbing back up.

additional_quotes.text: Register the morning of if needed - pop over, get your wristband, information, and placard, then head to the start line.
There was an expo in the days before with live music - a cool touch for families or non-cyclists who want something bike-related to do.
It's cold in the morning around 50-55 degrees, but once the sun comes out it gets really warm - it's summer in Big Bear, the snow capital of Southern California.

rider_intel.search_text: Tour de Big Bear is a 100-mile grand fondo in Big Bear Lake, California with 8,000+ feet of climbing. Riders describe the start as chaotic, zigzagging through neighborhoods before opening up. Key challenges include the first big climb at Onyx Pass around mile 43, which features a KOM/QOM timing segment from bottom to summit. The route goes around Big Bear Lake with beautiful scenery on the north and northeast sides. Cedar Falls serves as the turnaround point where riders still face 4,000 feet of climbing in the final 30 miles. Onyx Summit is described as particularly brutal. The event offers multiple distance options and includes aid stations throughout. Weather starts cold at 50-55 degrees in the morning but warms up significantly. Riders recommend starting near the front if experienced, arriving early for parking, and conserving energy for the challenging climbs.
